coredata.dc:title: Two-photon photodynamic therapy based on FRET using tumor-cell targeted riboflavin conjugated graphene quantum dot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Soleimany, Amir
Khoee, Sepideh
Dastan, Davoud
Shi, Zhicheng
Yu, Shengtao
Sarmento, Bruno
coredata.dc:description: The photodynamic therapy (PDT) is considered as a noninvasive and photo-controlled treatment for various cancers. However, its potential is not fully developed as current clinically approved photosensitizers (PSs) mainly absorb the light in the UV–visible region (less than 700 nm), where the depth of penetration is inadequate for reaching tumor cells under deeper tissue layers. Furthermore, the lack of specific accumulation capability of the conventional PSs in the tumor cells may cause serious toxicity and low treatment efficiency. To address these problems, riboflavin (Rf) conjugated and amine-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (am-N-GQD) are herein proposed. Rf functions as both photosensitizer and targeting ligand by indirect excitation through intra-particle fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) via two-photon (TP) excited am-N-GQD, to enhance the treatment depth, and further am-N-GQD-Rf accumulation in cancer cells using Rf transporter family (RFVTs) and Rf carrier proteins (RCPs). The one-photon (OP) and two-photon(TP)-PDT effect and cellular internalization ability of the am-N-GQD-Rf were investigated in vitro in different cancel cell lines. Besides the excellent cellular uptake as well TP-PDT capability, the superior biocompatibility of am-N-GQD-Rf in vitro makes it promising candidate in PDT.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amine-functionalized GQDs
Riboflavin
FRET
Two-photon-PDT
Tumor-targeted conjugate

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of am-N-GQDs via Hydrothermal Method
Determination of Relative PLQY of am-N-GQD
Two-Photon Induced Toxicity of am-N-GQD-Rf to KB Cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Iranian Ministry of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Croatia

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Iranian Ministry of Science and Technology awarded to University of Tehran . Also it was supported by the project Norte-01-0145-FEDER-000051 - “Cancer Research on Therapy Resistance: From Basic Mechanisms to Novel Targets”, supported by Norte Portugal Regional Operational Programme (NORTE 2020), under the PORTUGAL 2020 Partnership Agreement, through the European Regional Development Fund (FEDER).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the am-N-GQD-Rf delivery and singlet oxygen production mechanism.

Characterization of am-N-GQD: (a) TEM image of am-N-GQD (Insets show the HR-TEM image (right) and size distribution (left)); (b) FT-IR spectra of Rf, am-N-GQD and am-N-GQD-Rf; (c) full scan XPS spectrum of am-N-GQD and (d) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra (Ex:355 nm) of am-N-GQD.

Characterization of am-N-GQD-Rf: (a) UV–vis spectrum of Rf and fluorescence spectrum of am-N-GQD; (b) TEM image of am-N-GQD-Rf; (c) The fluorescence decay curves of am-N-GQD and am-N-GQD-Rf at 445 nm (Ex:355 nm); (d) Fluorescence spectrum of am-N-GQD, am-N-GQD-Rf and Rf (Ex: 355 nm), and (e) TP-Fluorescence spectrum (Ex: 800 nm).
CLSM photobleaching experiment of am-N-GQD-Rf, (a) before and (b) after bleaching of selected area using high intensity 458 nm laser for 5 min.

(a) and (b), UV–vis spectra of the ICG with and without am-N-GQD-Rf under 365 nm (3 mW.cm−2) irradiation over 32 min, respectively. (C) The normalized absorbance changes of the ICG at 780 nm for different solution under light irradiation during 32 min.

The CLSM images of KB cells incubated with am-N-GQD-Rf for 2 h (a) and 4 h (b). Green channel for Rf and blue channel for am-N-GQD. Scale bars show 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Cell viability of ARPE-19, KB and Caco-2 clone cell lines in the dark (a) and upon 365 nm (3 mW.cm−2 and 10 min) (b) treated with different Rf and nanoparticles doses.
The CLSM images of KB cells co-cultured without (control(A)) and with nanoparticles (am-N-GQD(B), am-N-GQD-Rf(C)) after laser irradiation (800 nm). The scale bar represents 100 μm.
Schematic synthesis of am-N-GQD and carboxylic acid-functionalized riboflavin (RF-SA)

head.ce:title: Two-photon photodynamic therapy based on FRET using tumor-cell targeted riboflavin conjugated graphene quantum dot

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Instituto de Investigação e Inovação em Saúde

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photodynamic therapy (PDT) is considered as a noninvasive and photo-controlled treatment for various cancers. However, its potential is not fully developed as current clinically approved photosensitizers (PSs) mainly absorb the light in the UV–visible region (less than 700 nm), where the depth of penetration is inadequate for reaching tumor cells under deeper tissue layers. Furthermore, the lack of specific accumulation capability of the conventional PSs in the tumor cells may cause serious toxicity and low treatment efficiency. To address these problems, riboflavin (Rf) conjugated and amine-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (am-N-GQD) are herein proposed. Rf functions as both photosensitizer and targeting ligand by indirect excitation through intra-particle fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) via two-photon (TP) excited am-N-GQD, to enhance the treatment depth, and further am-N-GQD-Rf accumulation in cancer cells using Rf transporter family (RFVTs) and Rf carrier proteins (RCPs). The one-photon (OP) and two-photon(TP)-PDT effect and cellular internalization ability of the am-N-GQD-Rf were investigated in vitro in different cancel cell lines. Besides the excellent cellular uptake as well TP-PDT capability, the superior biocompatibility of am-N-GQD-Rf in vitro makes it promising candidate in PDT.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past three decades, photodynamic therapy (PDT) has been proposed and used as a noninvasive, photo-controlled and spatially selective treatment strategy for various cancers in clinics. [1–3] In PDT, a light-activable drug called photosensitizer (PS) can absorb the light with appropriate wavelength to reach its singlet excited state (S1), which can then either regain its ground state (i.e., by thermal decay or fluorescence emission) or transform to its excited triplet state (T1) through intersystem crossing (ISC). Consequently, PS at triplet excited state can either transfer energy to the surrounding oxygen and convert it into the cytotoxic singlet oxygen (1O2) (Type II pathway), that is accounted as the dominant path, or generate free radicals via transferring an electron or hydrogen to the adjacent biological substrate, such as nucleic acids, proteins and water, which results in H2O2, O2•− and •OH production (Type I pathway). [4–8] In general, PDT treatment efficiency is highly dependent on the amount of light absorption by the accumulated PSs within tumor cells. [9] Despite great benefits and progress in this area, it is still not at the desired point due to two major limitations including mainly absorption of the current clinically approved PSs in the UV–visible region, where the depth of penetration is insufficient to reach tumor cells in deeper tissue layers, beside, the nonspecific accumulation of them, which may cause unpredictable toxicity as well as low treatment efficiency. [10–12] Whereas, synthesis of new near-infrared (NIR)-excitable or/and targeting agent conjugated small molecule PSs may circumvent the narrow penetration of the UV–visible and improve specific uptake. This results in increased PDT clinical value, overcoming the difficulties associated with complex organic synthesis. [12] Accordingly, scientists have offered many solutions based on nanotechnology including using nanocarriers to improve the tumor uptake and circulation time of the conjugated or loaded PSs, or designing organic or inorganic nanomaterials that have ROS generation ability upon NIR irradiation. [10,13] Furthermore, indirect excitation of the PSs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), when they are at an appropriate distance (1–10 nm) from an upconversion specie, which can generate high-energy (UV–visible) photons by absorbing two or more low-energy (NIR) photons through a nonlinear optical process is a promising way to extend the excitation wavelength of the PSs from the UV–visible to the NIR to increase the treatment depth and endow more spatial precision. On the grounds, it results in killing the underlying tumor cells with inducing less damage to the surrounding normal cells. [14,15]

Owning to high two-photon absorption cross-sections (TPAC), excellent water solubility, good photostability as well as desired biocompatibility, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted great attention in the nanomedicine field especially in the phototherapy and bioimaging as an emerging nanomaterial. [16,17] Moreover, the optical and electronic characteristics of the GQDs can be improved through facile surface functionalization and heteroatom doping by simply adding suitable reagents during the GQD preparation at high temperature, also owe to excellent anti-Stokes emission due to their high TPAC, they represent promising applications as the donor in the FRET-based two-photon excitation (TPE) PDT. [18,19] However, several studies on PSs-conjugated QDs for FRET based TP-PDT have been reported. Nevertheless, they are suffering from multistep preparation pathway and lack of selective uptake, which restrict them to be fully developed as practical PSs. [18,20–22]

Riboflavin (Rf), a water-soluble vitamin, one of the B-group vitamins (generally called Vitamin B2) is an essential precursor of the redox cofactors, flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD). [23] Recently, the overexpression of the Rf transporter family (RFVT1, RFVT2, and RFVT3) were demonstrated in squamous cell carcinoma, liver, prostate, and breast cancers. [24] Also elevated level of Rf carrier proteins (RCPs) were reported in blood and tumor tissues of patients with these cancers, which make RFVTs and RCPs promising biomarkers for tumor detection, and a desirable tool for targeted drug delivery by using Rf as a ligand for active targeting. [24–27] In addition, Rf 1O2 generation potential upon UV-blue illumination in bacterial and cellular PDT (one-photo (OP) and TP) have been investigated. [28–31] This combination of practical features, excellent biocompatibility, safety and superior availability encouraged us to design a nanoparticle, in which Rf acts as a photosensitizer and targeting ligand, simultaneously.

Based on the above survey, we have synthesized high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY 70%) amine-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (am-N-GQDs) via a simple hydrothermal route, followed by grafting of Riboflavin-Succinate (Rf-SA) to its surface through EDC/NHS coupling reaction to form am-N-GQD-Rf. Rf is selected as FRET acceptor owing to large overlap of its absorbance spectrum with the emission spectrum of am-N-GQD and active targeting potential to the cancer cells. In this system, am-N-GQD-Rf can accumulate in cancer cells mediated by RFVTs and RCPs, then can indirectly be excited during NIR (800 nm) irradiation through intra-particle FRET by TP-excited am-N-GQD (Fig. 1 ). In vitro OP and TP-PDT effect and cellular uptake efficiency of this conjugate were investigated to examine mentioned features as a promising PS. Although there are several studies on Rf as PS or targeting agent separately, [24,25,27,29–32] but to the best of our knowledge, there is no any report on the application of Rf as both PS and targeting ligand conjugated to high PLQY am-N-GQDs.

All chemicals and solvents were purchased from commercial sources and used as received. Citric acid (CA), Riboflavin (Rf), diethylenetriamine (DETA), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), Indocyanine green (ICG), 4-(dimethylamino)pyridine (DMAP), succinic anhydride (SA), quinine sulfate (QS), sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH) and 3-(4,5-Dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were received from Sigma-Aldrich. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. Acetone and diethyl ether (DEE) were supplied from Fluka. The Deionized (DI) water was obtained by a Milli-Q A10 purification system (Millipore) with 18.1 MΩ cm−1.

Lambda 35 (Perkin Elmer) spectrophotometer was used to study UV–Vis absorption spectra. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Bio Tek Synergy MX microplate reader. FT-IR spectra were obtained using a Perkin Elmer Frontier in the ATR mode. TEM was carried out after drying 5 μL of dilute nanoparticles solution on a copper grid 300 mesh (EMS-USA) using Zeiss-EM10C. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) spectra were recorded with a Thermo scientific XPS using Al K-alpha monochromatic. Crystalline structure of the nanoparticle was analyzed using a D8Advance, Bruker X-ray diffractometer. 13C NMR spectra was recorded with a Varian-INOVA (500 MHz).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of am-N-GQDs via Hydrothermal Method
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mmol CA (0.21 g) and 3 mmol DETA (0.34 g) were dissolved into 5 mL Milli-Q water, and kept stirring until obtaining a clear solution. Next, the solution was transferred into a 20 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Afterward, the sealed hydrothermal autoclave was heated up to 180 °C in an electric oven for 6 h. The obtained mixture was filtered via a 0.22 μm cellulose acetate syringe filter to remove the large-sized particles. The final product was collected by twice washing the solution with abundant ethanol, followed by centrifuging at 10000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the supernatant was removed, and the residue was redispersed into Milli-Q water to obtain stock solution and stored in a dark-colored vial at 4 °C.

500 mg Riboflavin (1.3 mmol), 320 mg DMAP (2.6 mmol), and 265 mg SA (2.6 mmol) were stirred in 25 mL DMF at 70 °C for 24 h. After cooling down at the ambient condition, the reaction mixture was filtered to discard residue and was poured dropwise into diethyl ether under vigorous stirring; the precipitate was filtered and dried under reduced pressure. Afterward, the crude product was recrystallized from acetone to obtain pure Rf-SA (Yield: 85%).

EDC (10 mg in 100 μL DI water) and NHS (10 mg in 100 μL DI water) were added to Rf-SA solution (4 mg in 200 μL DMF), and incubated at room temperature for 30 min to activate the carboxylic acid groups. It was poured into 1 mL of am-N-GQD (20 mg.mL−1) in DI water and was stirred at RT for 12 h. Then, the mixture was purified via dialysis (dialysis membrane; MW cut-off 1000 Da) for 4 days. The nanoparticle solution was stored at 4 °C for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Relative PLQY of am-N-GQD
ce:section.ce:para:
PLQY of am-N-GQD was measured relatively by using a 0.1 N H2SO4 aqueous solution of quinine sulfate (QY = 0.557) as standard. The QY was calculated according to the equation:
Qs = Qr
I s
×
A x
×
ɳ S
2
I x
×
A s
×
ɳ X
2
where, the subscripts S and X refer to standard and sample, also; I is the integrated emission intensity; A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and ɳ is the refractive index of the solvent. am-N-GQD and Quinine sulfate samples were prepared with appropriate concentrations where the maximum absorbance values at 355 nm were between 0 and 0.1. QY was evaluated by comparing the integrated PL intensity vs absorbance curves from the PL spectra.

Singlet oxygen generation was detected by the UV–vis absorption spectra of the 1O2 probe, indocyanine green (ICG). In this method, am-N-GQD-Rf (1 mg.mL−1), am-N-GQD (0.75 mg.mL−1, the same concentration in am-N-GQD-Rf), and Rf (0.25 mg.mL−1, the same concentration in am-N-GQD-Rf) were used. 10 mM ICG was homogeneously mixed with the solutions and irradiated by a UV-LED (365 nm, 3 mW.cm−2) for 32 min. The UV–vis spectra of mixture were recorded at different intervals. The control experiment was performed with 10 mM ICG under the same condition.

For the photo-bleaching experiment, 50 μL of the am-N-GQD-Rf solution was dropped onto a coverslip and dried under vacuum. Acceptor (Rf) was bleached by a 458 nm laser at 95% intensity for 6 min using CLSM. Finally, the sample was imaged with 405 and 458 nm laser at 10% intensity.

KB cells were incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 in a coverslip-loaded 24-well plate at a density of 2 × 105 cells/well in DMEM (supplemented with 10% FBS, 1% penicillin/streptomycin (Biowest,) and 1% Non-Essential Amino Acids (Gibco)) before the experiment. After 24 h, the old medium was discarded, and cells were washed three times with PBS 1×. Then, 500 μL am-N-GQD-Rf (100 μg.mL−1 in DMEM) was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for either 2 or 4 h. Next, cells were washed with PBS 1× three times to remove un-uptaken nanoparticles followed by fixation using 4% paraformaldehyde. Lastly, CLSM was used to detect the cellular uptake of the nanoparticle.

MTT assay was utilized to study the cytotoxicity of Riboflavin (Rf), am-N-GQDs, and am-N-GQD-Rf on the growth of the KB, Caco-2, and ARPE-19 cell lines. Cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 2 × 104 cells/well, including 200 μL of the fresh DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS, 1% penicillin/streptomycin (Biowest,) and 1% Non-Essential Amino Acids (Gibco) 24 h before the experiment. After removing the medium and washing twice with 200 μL PBS 1X, cells were treated with 200 μL of fresh medium containing different concentrations of Rf, am-N-GQD, am-N-GQD-Rf (3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50 and 100 μg.mL−1) for 48 h. Then, the cells were washed twice with 200 μL PBS 1×, followed by adding 200 μL MTT (0.5 mg.mL−1 in supplemented medium) to each well. The cells were incubated for 4 h before removing and replacing the MTT solution with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). After 30 min of incubation in dark media, the optical density was measured using a microplate reader at 570 nm. The same procedure was employed for the photo-cytotoxicity, while before adding MTT, the cells were irradiated by UV light (365 nm, 3 mW.cm−2) for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two-Photon Induced Toxicity of am-N-GQD-Rf to KB Cells
ce:section.ce:para: KB cells were seeded in a 35 mm confocal dish at density of 2 × 105 cells per well in DMEM complete medium (at 37 °C and 5% CO2). After 24 h, the cells were incubated with Rf (100 μg.mL−1), am-N-GQD (100 μg.mL−1) and am-N-GQD-Rf (100 μg.mL−1) for another 24 h, and subsequently were washed three times with PBS 1× to remove un-uptaken nanoparticles. Afterward, they were irradiated using a femtosecond laser (Ti-Sapphire, 800 nm, 80 MHz, 90 fs and 200 mW.cm−2) for 5 min, followed by incubation with propidium iodide (PI, 10 μM) for 15 min. After washing the cells, fluorescence images were captured using CLSM. Control was prepared with the same procedure without nanoparticle incubation to check the effect of the laser on the cells' viability.

High PLQY (76%) and amine-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (am-N-GQDs) as promising energy donor were prepared by a one-pot and bottom-up hydrothermal route employing citric acid as carbon source and DETA as nitrogen source as described previously with minor modifications (Scheme 1 ). [33] Fig. 2a depicts the TEM image of as-obtained am-N-GQDs, which reveals well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles with a Gaussian distribution of lateral size ranging from ∼3.4 to 6.6 nm. The average lateral size of am-N-GQDs was determined as 4.9 nm from statistical evaluation of over 100 particles using ImageJ software (Fig. 2a, inset). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image exhibits a well-resolved lattice fringe pattern with a lattice space of 0.35 nm, which is assigned to the (002) plane of graphite (Fig. 2a, inset). Similarly, the broad peak around 25.2° in the XRD pattern of am-N-GQDs confirmed its crystalline nature, which is correlated with the (002) plane detected by HRTEM (Supporting Information, Fig. S1). [33,34]

Then, the surface and elemental composition of am-N-GQDs were studied by FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS. Fig. 2b shows the FT-IR spectrum of am-N-GQD. The broad bands between ∼3200 and 3600 cm−1 correspond to the stretch vibrations of OH and NH. The bands obtained around 1115 and 1556 cm−1 result from CO and aromatic CC ring stretching vibrations, accordingly. [34] Other bands that appeared around 1250 and 1380 cm−1 are related to CN in-plane and N-C=O stretching. The shoulder around 1648 cm−1 corresponds to the CO in the amide (O=C-NH) vibrational absorption band. [33]

Furthermore, predominant C 1 s (284 eV), N 1 s (400 eV), and O 1 s (532 eV) peaks were observed in the full scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c), indicating a high extent of N doping in the structure. The C 1 s XPS spectrum was deconvoluted into four Gaussian peaks at 284.9, 285.9, 287.6, and 288.7 eV, which were attributed accordingly to graphitic sp2 C(C-C/C=C), sp3 carbon C (CC, CO, and CN), CO, and C(O)-O functional groups (Supporting Information, Fig. S2a). Fig. S2b (Supporting Information) shows the N 1 s XPS spectrum, which is divided into two peaks at 399.51, and 400.93 eV attributed to pyrrolic and graphitic N, respectively. Also, quinone and carbonyl (C=O) peaks located at 530.7 and 531.5 eV were appeared in the O 1 s spectrum (Supporting Information, Fig. S2c). [33,34] Altogether, graphene structure, in-situ N doping and amine functionalization of am-N-GQD were corroborated by FT-IR and XPS spectra.

UV–Vis and FL spectroscopy were evaluated to study the optical properties of the am-N-GQD. In the UV–Vis absorbance spectrum two characteristic maximum absorbances were observed at 240 nm (the π-π* transition of CC of sp2 C domain) and 355 nm (n–π* transitions of CO and conjugated C-N/C=N) due to the nitrogen doping in the am-N-GQD nanoparticle (Fig. 2d). [22] The FL emission spectrum of am-N-GQDs appeared as a broad emission band at about 445 nm, which is excellent for a FRET donor, under 355 nm excitation. Further, the emission spectra show a slight shift at different excitation wavelengths (320–400 nm) (Fig. S3, Supporting Information), which is evidence of the uniformly in the nanoparticles size and surface state of sp2 domains. [33] Moreover, the PL quantum yield of the am-N-GQDs in an aqueous solution was obtained as high as 76.83% using the comparative method, which involves the use of quinine sulfate (quantum yield 0.524) as the fluorescence standard (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). [35]

Rf is selected as the photosensitizer because of 1) the large overlap of its absorbance spectrum with the emission spectrum of am-N-GQD (Fig. 3a), which is required for excitement through FRET, [14] and 2) its active targeting potential for cancer cells, which makes conjugated nanoparticles able to accumulate in cancer cells using RFVTs and RCPs. [24] Firstly, carboxylic acid functionalized riboflavin (Rf-COOH) was synthesized using succinic acid (Scheme 1, Fig. S5, Supporting Information). [36] Afterward, it was activated through EDC/NHS chemistry to obtain riboflavin functionalized nanoparticle after mixing with am-N-GQDs. Finally, the solution was purified using a 1000 Da MWCO dialysis membrane to remove byproducts and un-bounded Rf. Rf conjugation efficiency (25% (w/w)) was determined by measuring un-bounded Rf using UV–vis spectroscopy.

TEM images reveal that the average size of the am-N-GQD-Rf (5.8 nm) (Fig. 3b) is somewhat larger than that of am-N-GOD (4.9 nm) (Fig. 2a), owing to the coupling of Rf. Besides, FT-IR spectroscopy was used to confirm the Rf coupling, as demonstrated in Fig. 2b, where a more pronounced band at 1648 cm−1 can be found for the am-N-GQD-Rf when compared to the am-N-GQD, which is related to the amide CO. [37] On top of that, in the conjugated form, characteristic bands of raw Rf in the region 800–1500 cm−1 emerged. In the UV–vis spectra of am-N-GQD-Rf, am-N-GQD, and Rf characteristic maximum absorption bands were appeared at 365, 355 and 445 nm, respectively, where a slight red-shift in the UV–Vis absorbance of the am-N-GQD was shown after Rf conjugation (Fig. S6, Supporting Information).

The optical properties of am-N-GQD and am-N-GQD-Rf are considered to evaluate the intraparticle FRET between am-N-GQD and Rf in the conjugated nanoparticle (am-N-GQD-Rf). The fluorescence decay curves with a bi-exponential fit for am-N-GQD and am-N-GQD-Rf at 445 nm (Ex; 355 nm) are presented in Fig. 3c. Accordingly, the average lifetime of am-N-GQD (donor) was 16.6 ns (ns), and it shortened to 6.8 ns in Rf-conjugated form (am-N-GQD-Rf), which confirmed an efficient FRET with 60% efficiency determined by Eq. 2:
(2)
E = 1 −
τ
D − A
τ D
where, τD-A and τD are the average lifetimes of the am-N-GQD before and after Rf conjugation, respectively. Furthermore, the emission spectra of the am-N-GQD-Rf, Rf (with an equivalent concentration of Rf in am-N-GQD-Rf) and am-N-GQD (with an equivalent concentration of am-N-GQD in am-N-GQD-Rf) were recorded under OPE (355 nm) and TPE (800 nm). As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the emission intensity of the am-N-GQD-Rf at 445 nm decreased considerably compared to the am-N-GQD; meanwhile, increased at 520 nm compared to the equivalent free Rf. Further, emerging the Rf emission under TPE in the am-N-GQD-Rf is another evidence of FRET in this conjugate, because of low TPACS of the Rf, its direct excitation through the TP-irradiation is not possible, accordingly, the emission is related to indirect-excitation of it via am-N-GQD (as shown Fig. 3e). On the other hand, solution color upon 365 nm LED irradiation changed from blue to green (Fig. 3d, inset) for am-N-GQD and am-N-GQD-Rf, respectively, which is perfectly correlated with the results of the FL spectroscopy, that all specify FRET phenomenon. Moreover, detection of the FL intensity of the donor (am-N-GQD) during acceptor (Rf) bleaching using CLSM is a reliable way to monitor intraparticle FRET processes. Therefore, a high-intensity 458 nm laser was used to bleach acceptor (Rf) in the selected regions, where the green (Rf emission) fluorescence of the am-N-GQD-Rf was converted to the blue (am-N-GQD emission) after Rf bleaching because there was no energy acceptor (Rf), which is another evidence of energy transfer between am-N-GQD and Rf (Fig. 4 ).

Next, to study the potential of am-N-GQD-Rf NPs for PDT, singlet oxygen generation, the main cytotoxic specie in PDT, upon OP (365 nm, 3 mW.cm−2) irradiation was evaluated using a singlet oxygen probe, i.e., indocyanine green (ICG). [38] Decomposition of ICG by singlet oxygen resulted in a decrease in the ICG absorbance at 780 nm, which was a quantitative method to compare the singlet oxygen production rates of am-N-GQD-Rf (Fig. 5a), am-N-GQD (Fig. S7a, Supporting Information), and Rf (Fig. S7b, Supporting Information) in the identical condition. As shown in Fig. 5c, the ICG absorbance at 780 nm decreases rapidly to 5% in the PBS buffer containing am-N-GQD-Rf after 32 min irradiation. This reduction was 64% and 75% for am-N-GQD (the same concentration in the am-N-GQD-Rf) and Rf (the same concentration in the am-N-GQD-Rf), respectively, while it was negligible (to 93%) for the ICG solution under the same irradiation condition (Fig. 5b). Significant singlet oxygen production of the am-N-GQD-Rf compared to am-N-GQD and Rf show an excellent synergic effect, which is an excellent outcome for this conjugation.

As mentioned before, Rf-receptor overexpression in some cancer cells can enhance its uptake and makes it promising to be used as a targeting agent for these cancer cells. Therefore, KB (mouth epidermal carcinoma) cell lines, as overexpressed riboflavin-receptor cell line, [39–41] were treated with am-N-GQD-Rf to investigate cellular uptake of the nanoparticle using CLSM. Fluorescence intensity of the Rf was used to compare uptake in different incubation times (2 and 4 h). As illustrated in Fig. 6 , a significant cell labeling observed after 4 h incubation, which confirms efficient cell entering of the nanoparticle toward Rf-receptor overexpressed cell line.

MTT cytotoxicity assessments of KB, Caco-2, and ARPE-19 cell lines incubated with Rf, am-N-GQD, and am-N-GQD-Rf in dark and also, photocytotoxicity (365 nm, 3 mW.cm−2, and 10 min) were performed to evaluate biocompatibility and OP-PDT potential of the am-N-GQD-Rf in-vitro. As demonstrated in Fig. 7 , more than 80% viability even at a high concentration (100 μg.mL−1) after 48 h incubation in dark specifies superior biocompatibility of nanoparticles. Interestingly, upon irradiation at 100 μg.mL−1 of am-N-GQD-Rf viability decreased to 48, 30, and 14% for ARPE-19, KB, and Caco-2, respectively, that demonstrates excellent OP-PDT potential of this conjugate. Higher phototoxicity against KB and Caco-2 clone as the cancerous and overexpressed Rf receptor cell lines compare to the healthy ARPE-19 cells exhibit the acceptable application of this conjugate as PS.

Finally, TP-PDT was also carried out on the KB cell line as overexpressed RFVTs to evaluate the nanoparticle potential in this strategy. Therefore, propidium iodide (PI) was employed to labeling the dead cells upon femto-second laser (Ti-Sapphire, 800 nm, 80 MHz, 90 fs and 200 mW.cm−2, 5 min) irradiation. Laser effect on cells was examined through nanoparticle-free cells under same procedure. As represented in Fig. 8 , Rf and am-N-GQD illustrated a negligible cytotoxicity compare to extreme effect by am-N-GQD-Rf upon laser irradiation, which can be related to the lower content of TPAC for Rf and insufficient singlet oxygen quantum yield of the am-N-GQD, respectively. The superior phototoxicity of the am-N-GQD-Rf under laser irradiation can be related to the indirect activation of the Rf through FRET and also synergic effect, which was observed in singlet oxygen production under UV irradiation.

We designed and synthesized tumor-targeted and TP-excitable am-N-GQD-based PS using one-step Rf conjugation as the targeting agent and PS simultaneously. An adequate overlap of the Rf absorbance spectrum with the emission spectrum of the am-N-GQD makes its indirect excitation possible through FRET. Moreover, superior synergic effect in singlet oxygen generation was found for this conjugation. Accordingly, based on surface functionalization with Rf, it can effectively be accumulated in KB and Caco-2 clone cells, in which RFVTs are overexpressed on their surface, and induce cell death followed by OP (365 nm) and TP (800 nm) irradiation. Low cost and excellent biocompatibility of used sources, simple and fast synthesis approach, tumor affinity, and high singlet oxygen production capability upon NIR irradiation endow am-N-GQD-Rf with promising potential to pave the path for future studies.
XRD, high-resolution XPS and emission spectra of am-N-GQD, 13C NMR Spectrum of the riboflavin succinate.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Iranian Ministry of Science and Technology awarded to University of Tehran. Also it was supported by the project Norte-01-0145-FEDER-000051 - “Cancer Research on Therapy Resistance: From Basic Mechanisms to Novel Targets”, supported by Norte Portugal Regional Operational Programme (NORTE 2020), under the PORTUGAL 2020 Partnership Agreement, through the European Regional Development Fund (FEDER).
